--- SOURCE 1: Effective Stakeholder Engagement in Agriculture - Number Analytics ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/effective-stakeholder-engagement-in-agriculture

SUMMARY:
A: Stakeholder engagement is important in agriculture because it helps to ensure that the needs and interests of all stakeholders are considered and addressed , which is critical for the long-term sustainability and success of agricultural projects and initiatives.

--- SOURCE 2: Agriculture development through multi-stakeholder partnerships in ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X

SUMMARY:
Multi-stakeholder partnerships (MSPs), which involve the collective action of various institutions such as governments, private companies, NGOs, and donors, have become a popular approach for developing agricultural value chains to improve the sustainability of food systems. The objective of this study was to systematically review the literature on MSPs in agricultural development, specifically examining the various concepts employed and assessing their realized impacts on farmers, focusing on developing countries. Multi-stakeholder partnerships (MSPs) in agriculture denote a collective action of various institutions such as government, private companies, NGOs, and donors intended to support developing and improving agricultural value chains (Bäckstrand, 2006; Dentoni et al., 2018; Tinarwo et al., 2018).

FULL CONTENT:

Review

Agriculture development through multi-stakeholder partnerships in developing countries: A systematic literature review
==============================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Maryono Maryono a b, Aditya Marendra Killoes a c, Rajendra Adhikari a, Ammar Abdul Aziz a

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2023.103792](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2023.103792 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0308521X2300197X&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Multi-stakeholder partnerships (MSPs) have become a popular approach for developing agricultural value chains. 
*   •This study examines the various concepts employed and assesses their realized impacts on farmers. 
*   •We found that a lack of consensus on the defining characteristics of such partnerships and positive impacts were reported. 
*   •This paper presents a comprehensive overview of the diverse practices of MSPs and analyses their contributions. 

Abstract
--------

### CONTEXT

Multi-stakeholder partnerships (MSPs), which involve the [collective action](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/collective-action "Learn more about collective action from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of various institutions such as governments, private companies, NGOs, and donors, have become a popular approach for developing agricultural value chains to improve the sustainability of food systems. However, the growing body of literature presents a range of terms, concepts, and definitions related to MSPs, potentially causing confusion and obscuring the distinctions between different types of partnerships. While the potential benefits of MSPs have been extensively explored in the literature, there remains a need to investigate their realized impacts across regions to enable a more robust synthesis.

### OBJECTIVE

The objective of this study was to systematically review the literature on MSPs in agricultural development, specifically examining the various concepts employed and assessing their realized impacts on farmers, focusing on developing countries.

### METHODS

We systematically reviewed the literature on MSPs and identified 147 studies dealing with a different type of MSPs and 79 studies reporting the impact.

### RESULTS AND CONCLUSIONS

We found that twelve terms have been used to describe MSPs. However, a single MSP initiative may be referred to by different names in various documents, indicating a lack of consensus on the defining characteristics of such partnerships. Positive impacts were reported on farmers' economic, social, and technological aspects, as well as environmental adaptation practices to address [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Nevertheless, the reported impacts tend to focus primarily on economic, technological, and social dimensions while giving less attention to environmental aspects.

### SIGNIFICANCE

This paper presents, firstly, a comprehensive overview of the diverse practices of MSPs in developing countries. Secondly, it analyses the contributions made by MSPs across various commodities and regions in the development of agricultural value chains.

Keywords
--------

Systematic review

Multi-stakeholder partnerships

Impacts

Developing countries

1. Introduction
---------------

Poverty, [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and sustainability are critical issues in agricultural development, particularly in developing countries ([Timmer, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0380); [Demont and Rutsaert, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0070); [Viteri Salazar et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0410); [Turner et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0400)). The complexity of agricultural development problems requires value chain actors and stakeholders to interact and collaborate to achieve greater development outcomes ([Sartas et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0310)). Multi-stakeholder partnerships (MSPs) in [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") denote a [collective action](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/collective-action "Learn more about collective action from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of various institutions such as government, private companies, NGOs, and donors intended to support developing and improving agricultural value chains ([Bäckstrand, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0030); [Dentoni et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0080); [Tinarwo et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0385)). A growing body of literature suggests that MSPs can be a practical approach to solving economic, social, and environmental problems to achieve impact at scale ([van Tulder et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0395); [de Bakker et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0035); [Totin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0390)). The approach has also been considered fundamental to achieving the sustainable development goals (SDGs) of the United Nations 2030 Agenda ([Rein and Stott, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0295); [Eweje et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0100)).

The emergence of MSPs over the last two decades has attracted the attention of academics and practitioners as they seek to understand different practices under the broad umbrella of MSPs ([Barzola Iza et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0040)). It is being studied from multiple and different streams of disciplinary angles and stakeholders' viewpoints ([van Tulder et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0395); [Barzola Iza et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0040)), thus leading to a broad range of terms used to refer to the different forms of MSPs ([Narrod et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0250); [Cavatassi et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0060); [Dentoni and Peterson, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0075); [Köhne, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0185); [Thiele et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0375); [Téno and Cadilhon, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0365); [Hermans et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0130); [Totin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0390)). Researchers often adopt terms, concepts, and definitions from different disciplines with little reference to existing ones, leading to confusion and blurring the boundaries between the different types of MSPs ([van Tulder et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0395)). Therefore, clarifying and scrutinizing the different practices and terminology used under the MSPs framework is necessary.

Various organizations have implemented MSPs. Since the early 2000s, international development organizations have promoted partnerships between public and private actors to provide public goods ([Rein and Stott, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0295); [van Tulder et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0395)). As a result, it has seen the top one hundred largest firms in the world involved in partnerships with ‘non-market’ actors to maintain long-term competitive advantage ([Dentoni and Peterson, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0075); [van Tulder et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0395); [de Bakker et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0035)). Research organizations used MSPs to enhance the impact of research further than its initial development ([Sartas et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0310); [Schut et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0325)). For instance, using MSPs in [agricultural innovation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/agricultural-innovation "Learn more about agricultural innovation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") systems is expected to create an enabling environment for institutional and technological innovation ([Sartas et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0310)). Numerous development agencies, particularly in Africa, also promoted MSPs as drivers of change to promote access to knowledge and resources, enhance learning, and negotiate interest ([Adekunle and Fatunbi, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0015); [Adekunle et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0020); [Van Paassen et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0405)).

While the potential theoretical benefits of the MSPs have been extensively discussed in the literature, the realized impacts still need to be examined, highlighting the challenges in monitoring and evaluation in practice ([van Tulder et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0395)). It also implies that greater attention is needed to identify and evaluate the impacts of MSPs. Some studies reported that MSPs had helped small-scale producers' livelihood through improvement in income ([Cavatassi et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0060); [Thiele et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0370)) and capacity building ([Narrod et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0250); [Totin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0390)). Experience from Africa indicates that MSPs' benefits depend on the crops grown and farmers' access to fundamental resources such as land ([Adam et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0010)). There is a need for a more rigorous synthesis and evaluation of the impacts of MSPs implemented across regions to inform adaptive management of the approach to understanding better ‘what works’ in the process design and facilitation. More evidence on the impact of MSPs is also needed for policymakers and donor organizations to guide future development and implementation. This will facilitate the identification of interventions that can effectively improve the livelihood of smallholder producers and promote environmentally friendly agricultural practices in response to [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

While there are studies that have reviewed the contributions of MSPs, most of them tend to focus on a particular type of MSP or have been applied to a specific country or limited to a few regions ([Hussain and Perera, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0145); [Poulton and Macartney, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0290); [Ponnusamy, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0280); [Schut et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0320); [de Bakker et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0035); [Mangeni, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0210); [Barzola Iza et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0040); [van Ewijk and Ros-Tonen, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0105)). The most recent systematic review focused on the contribution of MSPs in sub-Saharan Africa and on those that enable knowledge co-creation ([van Ewijk and Ros-Tonen, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0105)). Only some studies, if any, have adopted a systematic approach to analyze and synthesize the diversity of terminology used to reflect the adoption of MSPs. To fill the gap, we explore the diverse practices and evaluate the impact of MSPs by systematically reviewing the literature on the MSPs in agricultural development in developing countries. We analyze and synthesize the findings of 147 studies for the definitions of MSPs and their contribution to the economic, social, technological, and environmental aspects of smallholder producers in developing countries.

2. Methods
----------

We conducted this systematic review following the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) ([Moher et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0245)), which requires all steps in the review process to be transparent and well-documented ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#f0005)). Unlike a narrative literature review, it uses a clear and systematic method to identify, select, and analyze relevant documents from a set of formulated research questions and pre-specified inclusion criteria to minimize the potential for selection bias ([Moher et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0245); [Koutsos et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0190); [Snyder, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0340)). The systematic review enables us to identify the diverse practices in implementing MSPs and their contributions to agricultural development. It also identifies research gaps in the literature and possible areas for future research.

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0308521X2300197X-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (366KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0308521X2300197X-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (366KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0308521X2300197X-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. [PRISMA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/systematic-review "Learn more about PRISMA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") flow diagram.

### 2.1. Scope

A specific set of inclusion and exclusion criteria were developed, as presented in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#t0005). For inclusion, studies had to present a quantitative, qualitative, or conceptual analysis of the collaborative action of multi-stakeholders in agricultural development in developing countries. The quantitative studies included were based on statistical analyses, manipulative experiments, or studies that combined multiple quantitative methods. The conceptual analysis included reviews and theoretical studies.

Table 1. Inclusion and exclusion criteria set for the selection of documents.

| Inclusion | Exclusion |
| --- | --- |
| * •The document discussed and focused on the collaborative action of multi-stakeholders in agricultural development * •The document dealt with MSPs as a significant aspect of their analysis rather than only mentioning them in passing * •Studies on the agriculture sector in general: crops, aquaculture, and animal husbandry * •The study setting is in developing countries * •Written in the English language | * •The document discussed partnerships that have either minimal multi-stakeholder representation or do not rely on a multi-stakeholder model * •Studies conducted in developed countries * •Written in a non-English language * •The full document is not available |

We only included documents that discuss MSPs as a focus of their analysis rather than only stating them in passing. We excluded studies on other types of partnership under private regulation, such as business-driven initiatives with either insignificant multi-stakeholder representation or those that did not rely on multi-stakeholder arrangements, such as contract farming. We included general [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") studies on crops, aquaculture, and animal husbandry. Studies in developed countries were also excluded. We included journal articles and grey literature, such as conference proceedings, reports, theses, books, and book chapters. There were no restrictions applied to the publication date. Studies were only included if written in English, and full documents were available.

### 2.2. Search

We searched six relevant scientific databases, i.e. Web of Science, ABI/Inform, Scopus, EconLit/EBSCOhost, AgEcon, and ScienceDirect Freedom Collection, for peer-reviewed articles, conference proceeding, reports, thesis, book, and book chapters published until 30 June 2020 with no restrictions applied on language and publication date and was updated on 18 January 2023. In addition, we also conducted hand searches of more specific databases and websites, as some of the documents on the topic were not indexed in an academic database. Therefore, we included six research organizations and donor websites that have conducted or supported research on the MSPs identified during the initial study, i.e. IRRI, CGIAR, FAO, IFPRI, USAID, and ACIAR. As [Hopewell et al. (2007)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#bb0135) suggested, we manually searched the reference lists of the documents included in the final list to identify other relevant documents not captured by our initial searches.

To retrieve relevant documents, we used a combination of the following search terms: “agriculture” and “multi-stakeholder partnership” vis-à-vis other related terms such as “public-private partnership” and “innovation platform”. We used explicitly designed search strings for each database and included alternative search terms where needed. The document selection process was completed in two steps iteratively. In the first step, the search string specified words related to agriculture (Search 1 in [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#t0010)), followed by the subsequent search using search string specified words related to multi-stakeholder partnership (Search 2 in [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#t0010)). The second step was a search of two inclusion terms linked by a Boolean operator (#1 AND #2). The search was unrestricted and included studies on different documents, the plot, farm, household, country, regional, and global scale. The search strings in [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#t0010) were specific to our research objectives and produced an initial database that reflects the available literature.

Table 2. Search strings used in the literature search.

| Search Number | Search string |
| --- | --- |
| #1 | TITLE: (Agri* OR Aquacultur* OR Crop* OR Dairy OR Farm* OR Fish* OR Flori* OR Fruit OR Grower OR Horticultur* OR Livestock OR Peasant OR Plant*OR Smallholder* OR “Small-holder*” OR “Small scale” OR Subsistence) |
| #2 | TITLE: (“MultiStakeholder” OR multi-stakeholder OR “Innovat* platform*” OR PPP OR “public private partner*” OR “public private collab*” OR “Stakeholder participat*” OR “Value chain collab*” OR “stakeholder collab*” OR “Stakeholder engagement”) |
| #1 AND #2 | TITLE: (Agri* OR Aquacultur* OR Crop* OR Dairy OR Farm* OR Fish* OR Flori* OR Fruit OR Grower OR Horticultur* OR Livestock OR Peasant OR Plant*OR Smallholder* OR “Smallholder*” OR “Small scale” OR Subsistence) AND (“Multi Stakeholder” OR multi-stakeholder OR “Innovat* platform*” OR PPP OR “public private partner*” OR “public private collab*” OR “Stakeholder participat*” OR “Value chain collab*” OR “stakeholder collab*” OR “Stakeholder engagement”) |

### 2.3. Screening

All documents identified during the search stage were screened based on the inclusion criteria. Studies that did not meet the inclusion criteria were excluded from the review. In the initial search, we identified a total of 2353 entries. All documents were then screened to meet the inclusion criteria. Covidence, a systematic review screening software, was used in the screening process. A total of 388 documents were excluded due to duplication. The screening process then continued with a review of the title and abstract. A total of 1668 documents were excluded as they were irrelevant to the topic. The multistage screening process produced 297 documents eligible for a full-text review. In addition, we manually searched the references of the remaining documents to identify any documents not captured during the initial search. This process identified 19 additional documents. The documents were then content-analyzed and selected according to the inclusion/exclusion criteria. This process resulted in the exclusion of 169 documents for the first research question and 237 documents for the second research question. The final screening process yielded 147 documents included in the analysis of the first research question to explore the diverse practices of MSPs, and 79 documents included to address the second research question to evaluate the impact of MSPs (See [Appendix A Supplementary data](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0308521X2300197X#st0175)).

### 2.4. Data extraction and analysis

Extracted data, piloted extensively, include bibliographic information, study location, design and methodology. For the first research question, we collated data on the characteristics of the MSPs. This included their type, objective, intervention, location, commodity, the initiator and partners involved. For the second research question, the data extraction focused on the reported impacts of the identified MSPs. The impacts were grouped into four broad categories: economic, social, technological, and environmental. The categories were not mutually exclusive, as impacts could be categorized into two or more categories. Microsoft Excel was used for data extraction and

enriched_points.expanded_content: Effective stakeholder collaboration ensures diverse interests are addressed, enhancing the sustainability and success of agricultural projects, as highlighted by Number Analytics.
Education and training empower stakeholders with necessary skills and knowledge, fostering innovation and improving practices, thus facilitating successful agricultural initiatives.
Creating a supportive ecosystem through multi-stakeholder partnerships enhances resource sharing and fosters collaboration, driving agricultural value chain improvements and sustainability.
